--- SOURCE 1: Detecting and mitigating bias in natural language processing ---
URL: https://www.brookings.edu/articles/detecting-and-mitigating-bias-in-natural-language-processing/

SUMMARY:
Unsupervised artificial intelligence (AI) models that automatically discover hidden patterns in natural language datasets capture linguistic regularities that reflect human biases, such as racism, sexism, and ableism.1 These unsupervised AI models, namely word embeddings, provide the foundational, general-purpose, numeric representation of language for machines to process textual data. Social media platforms automatically decide which users should be exposed to certain types of content present in political advertisements and information influence operations, based on personality characteristics predicted from their data.5 As researchers identify and measure the harmful side effects of NLP algorithms that incorporate biased models of language, regulation of algorithms and AI models can help alleviate the harmful downstream impacts of large-scale AI technologies.

**Editor's note:**
This report from The Brookings Institution’s Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technology (AIET) Initiative is part of [“AI and Bias,”](https://www.brookings.edu/series/ai-and-bias/)a series that explores ways to mitigate possible biases and create a pathway toward greater fairness in AI and emerging technologies.

Executive Summary
-----------------

Unsupervised artificial intelligence (AI) models that automatically discover hidden patterns in natural language datasets capture linguistic regularities that reflect human biases, such as racism, sexism, and ableism.1 These unsupervised AI models, namely word embeddings, provide the foundational, general-purpose, numeric representation of language for machines to process textual data.

Word embeddings identify the hidden patterns in word co-occurrence statistics of language corpora, which include grammatical and semantic information as well as human-like biases. Consequently, when word embeddings are used in natural language processing (NLP), they propagate bias to supervised downstream applications contributing to biased decisions that reflect the data’s statistical patterns. These downstream applications perform tasks such as information retrieval, text generation, machine translation, text summarization, and web search, in addition to consequential decision-making during resume screening for job candidate selection, university admissions automation, or essay grading. Word embeddings play a significant role in shaping the information sphere and can aid in making consequential inferences about individuals. Job interviews, university admissions, essay scores, content moderation, and many more decision-making processes that we might not be aware of increasingly depend on these NLP models.

Billions of people using the internet every day are exposed to biased word embeddings. However, no regulation is in place to audit these AI technologies that pose potential threats to equity, justice, and democracy. As a result, there is an urgent need for regulatory mechanisms, a diverse AI ethics workforce, and technical approaches to prevent AI technologies from accelerating its harmful side-effects.2

Understanding Bias in Natural Language Processing (NLP)
-------------------------------------------------------

Amazon’s automated resume screening for selecting the top job candidates turned out to be discriminating against women in 2015.3 Amazon used resume samples of job candidates from a 10-year period to train its recruitment models. This supervised downstream NLP application learned how to score candidates by computing the patterns in previous resume samples from Amazon and respective information regarding the success level of the job candidate. As a result, the trained model learned the historical trends associated with employment at Amazon by discovering linguistic patterns on resumes. Women were underrepresented in the training set collected from employees. Consequently, the resume screening model associated men and the linguistic signals on their resumes with successful employment at Amazon, whereas resumes of candidates which contained words associated with women were frequently discarded by the algorithm. The biased patterns learned by the model led to discrimination against female job candidates. Amazon soon abandoned the automated recruitment tool after they discovered the bias.

NLP applications’ biased decisions not only perpetuate historical biases and injustices, but potentially amplify existing biases at an unprecedented scale and speed. Future generations of word embeddings are trained on textual data collected from online media sources that include the biased outcomes of NLP applications, information influence operations, and political advertisements from across the web. Consequently, training AI models on both naturally and artificially biased language data creates an AI bias cycle that affects critical decisions made about humans, societies, and governments.

AI and NLP technologies are not standardized or regulated, despite being used in critical real-world applications. Technology companies that develop cutting edge AI have become disproportionately powerful with the data they collect from billions of internet users. These datasets are being used to develop AI algorithms and train models that shape the future of both technology and society. AI companies deploy these systems to incorporate into their own platforms, in addition to developing systems that they also sell to governments or offer as commercial services.

> “Technology companies that develop cutting edge AI have become disproportionately powerful with the data they collect from billions of internet users.”

With the lack of regulation and readily available bias auditing mechanisms, AI companies have not provided transparency in the everyday effects of the algorithms that they deploy in society. For example, Google’s machine translation algorithms convert the gender-neutral Turkish sentences “O bir profesör. O bir öğretmen” to the English sentences “He’s a professor. She is a teacher.” Facebook ran human subject experiments on its platform to study how to manipulate users’ emotions via biased text that induces associations of unpleasantness.4

Social media platforms automatically decide which users should be exposed to certain types of content present in political advertisements and information influence operations, based on personality characteristics predicted from their data.5 As researchers identify and measure the harmful side effects of NLP algorithms that incorporate biased models of language, regulation of algorithms and AI models can help alleviate the harmful downstream impacts of large-scale AI technologies.

Biases in word embeddings
-------------------------

In 2017, at Princeton University’s Center for Information Technology Policy, Joanna Bryson, Arvind Narayanan, and I developed methods demonstrating that word embeddings learn human-like biases from word co-occurrence statistics.6 When words representing concepts appear frequently with certain attributes, word embeddings learn to associate the concept with the co-occurring attributes. For example, sentences that contain words related to kitchen or arts tend to contain words related to women. However, sentences that contain career, science, and technology terms tend to contain words related to men. As a result, when machines are processing language to learn word embeddings, women, as a social group, appear in close proximity to words like family and arts relative to men; whereas, men, as a social group, appear in close proximity to career, science, and technology. We found that stereotypical associations exist for gender, race, age, and intersections among these characteristics. When these stereotypical associations propagate to downstream applications that present information on the internet or make consequential decisions about individuals, they disadvantage minority and underrepresented group members. As long as language corpora used to train NLP models contain biases, word embeddings will keep replicating historical injustices in downstream applications unless effective regulatory practices are implemented to deal with bias.

Racial bias in NLP
------------------

Studying biases in widely used word embeddings trained on a corpus of 800 billion words collected from the web reveals that names of African Americans tend to co-occur with unpleasant words. Measuring the relative association of names of African Americans vs. names of white people with pleasant and unpleasant words shows that the word embeddings contain negative associations for the concept of an African American social group due to the biased depiction of the group on the internet.7 These types of associations that reflect negative attitudes toward one social group are considered harmful and prejudiced. Similar negative associations are reflected for the elderly and people with disabilities. And women are often associated with family and literature, whereas men are associated with career and science. It is also worth noting that state-of-the-art language models generally capture the stereotypes and biases present in American culture, even though these NLP technologies are employed across the world.

[Ethical algorithm design should guide technology regulation](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/ethical-algorithm-design-should-guide-technology-regulation/)

Michael Kearns, Aaron Roth

January 13, 2020

[Fairness in algorithmic decision-making](https://www.brookings.edu/articles/fairness-in-algorithmic-decision-making/)

![Image 23: An image of Israeli soldiers is seen on a computer screen with colourful markings of a face recognition programming script, during a cyber security training course, called a Hackathon, at iNT Institute of Technology and Innovation, at a high-tech park in Beersheba, southern Israel August 28, 2017. Picture taken August 28, 2017. REUTERS/Amir Cohen - RC1DE502FAB0](https://www.brookings.edu/wp-content/uploads/2019/11/Cybersecurity_digital-trade_001.jpg?quality=75&w=500)

[Technology & Information](https://www.brookings.edu/topics/technology-innovation/) Fairness in algorithmic decision-making 
Mark MacCarthy

December 6, 2019

In 2004, a controlled study on labor market discrimination found that resumes that contain uniquely white names receive 50 percent more callbacks for interviews compared to resumes with uniquely African American names with the same qualifications.8 Using the job applicant names provided in the labor market discrimination study during bias quantification in word embeddings exposes strong negative associations with African Americans as a social group. While humans make consequential decisions about other humans on individual or collective bases, black-box NLP technologies make large-scale decisions that are deterministically biased. Accordingly, society faces a more significant and accelerated challenge compared to dealing with human decisionmakers as NLP is not regulated to promote equity and social justice.9

Gender bias in NLP
------------------

State-of-the-art large language models that learn dynamic context-dependent word embeddings, such as the multi-million-dollar model GPT-3, associates men with competency and occupations demonstrating higher levels of education in downstream NLP tasks.10 Many experts consider the text generated by GPT-3 as indistinguishable from human-generated text based on various criteria. Regardless, when prompted for language generation with the input “what is the gender of a doctor?” the first answer is, “Doctor is a masculine noun;” whereas, when prompted with “What is the gender of a nurse?” the first answer is, “It’s female.”

Moreover, word embeddings, either static or dynamic, associate the intersection of race and gender with the highest magnitude of disadvantaging bias.11 Like other AI algorithms that reflect the status quo, all social groups that are not made up of white men are represented as minority groups due to a lack of accurate and unbiased data to train word embeddings. For example, members of multiple minority groups, such as African American women, are strongly associated with various disadvantaging biases compared to the relatively less intense biases associated with their constituent minority groups, African Americans or women. The same strong and potentially harmful biased associations exist for Mexican American women as well. Consequently, propagation of social group bias in downstream NLP applications such as automated resume screening would not only perpetuate existing biases but potentially exacerbate harmful biases in society that will affect future generations.12

The problems of debiasing by social group associations
------------------------------------------------------

Word embedding debiasing is not a feasible solution to the bias problems caused in downstream applications since debiasing word embeddings removes essential context about the world. Word embeddings capture signals about language, culture, the world, and statistical facts. For example, gender debiasing of word embeddings would negatively affect how accurately occupational gender statistics are reflected in these models, which is necessary information for NLP operations. Gender bias is entangled with grammatical gender information in word

SUMMARY:
We propose a new debiasing framework that introduces binary classifiers between the auxiliary model and the main model, coined bias experts.

--- SOURCE 3: Bias and Fairness in Natural Language Processing - Medium ---
URL: https://medium.com/tr-labs-ml-engineering-blog/bias-and-fairness-in-natural-language-processing-7663a6d33932

SUMMARY:
Bias and Fairness in Natural Language Processing | by Navid Rekabsaz | Thomson Reuters Labs | Medium In what follows, I first provide a brief background on bias and fairness in NLP applications and explain the root causes and potential consequences of biases on models’ predictions. Bias in NLP was first reported in the form of gender bias in word embeddings [1] and have since been studied in various NLP models such as SLMs [2], and LLMs [3], as well as diverse applications such as Information Retrieval [5,6] and text classification [8]. These stereotypical associations encoded in the embedding level is a root cause of unfair and biased predictions of NLP models and should be taken into account depending on the context of downstream applications as discussed in the following.

FULL CONTENT:
Published Time: 2025-01-24T22:20:10.958Z

Bias and Fairness in Natural Language Processing | by Navid Rekabsaz | Thomson Reuters Labs | Medium

Bias and Fairness in Natural Language Processing
================================================

[![Image 3: Navid Rekabsaz](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*dn-u9ou1FCUzrTvOXAXqJA.png)](https://medium.com/@navid.rekabsaz_tr?source=post_page---byline--7663a6d33932---------------------------------------)

[Navid Rekabsaz](https://medium.com/@navid.rekabsaz_tr?source=post_page---byline--7663a6d33932---------------------------------------)

Follow

9 min read

·

Jan 24, 2025

[](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fvote%2Ftr-labs-ml-engineering-blog%2F7663a6d33932&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Ftr-labs-ml-engineering-blog%2Fbias-and-fairness-in-natural-language-processing-7663a6d33932&user=Navid+Rekabsaz&userId=42b57f2f0a02&source=---header_actions--7663a6d33932---------------------clap_footer------------------)

14

[](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F_%2Fbookmark%2Fp%2F7663a6d33932&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Ftr-labs-ml-engineering-blog%2Fbias-and-fairness-in-natural-language-processing-7663a6d33932&source=---header_actions--7663a6d33932---------------------bookmark_footer------------------)

Share

B ias and fairness have been a critical topic in Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Natural Language Processing (NLP), and a pillar of implementing and deploying trustworthy AI/NLP applications. In what follows, I first provide a brief background on bias and fairness in NLP applications and explain the root causes and potential consequences of biases on models’ predictions. This covers bias in word embeddings, Small Language Models (SLM) like BERT and RoBERTa, and Large Language Models (LLM) — the models widely used as the building blocks of various NLP applications. I then review the path to understand and address such societal biases in NLP models. While this article takes the examples from occupations and human resources domain, the discussed topics and the overall methods are directly transferrable to other application domains such as legal, tax, and news.

From Society to Encoded Model Biases
====================================

A large body of research in the last years raise awareness of and study the existence of societal biases in NLP models. Such biases are typically captured from the statistical regularities present in the underlying training data and are reflected in models’ predictions used in downstream applications. Bias in NLP was first reported in the form of gender bias in word embeddings [1] and have since been studied in various NLP models such as SLMs [2], and LLMs [3], as well as diverse applications such as Information Retrieval [5,6] and text classification [8].

![Image 4](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*40B8JdGSl2exjofTUj21og.jpeg)

Example of gender bias in language translation (NLP)

To observe a common case of societal biases in downstream NLP applications, the figure above shows the translations of a set of sentences from a grammatically gender-neutral language (Persian/Farsi in this example) to a gender-specific one (English). Gender-neutral languages like Farsi do not distinguish between masculine, feminine or neuter genders, and use the same pronouns without specifying the gender of the subject (as shown in the sentences on the left side of the figure). When translated to English, a machine translation system however needs to “decide” the target pronouns. As shown, the translation results in this example vary between _she_ and _he_, depending on the contexts of the sentences. While none of the mentioned occupations or adjectives in the contexts are gender-specific, the translated sentences reflect a biased perception, assuming that the _nurse_ should be a _she_, and the _manager_ a _he_!

![Image 5](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*3Guw908IFP1nXN2aV_3Gmg.jpeg)

Image from [6]

To put this topic in a big picture, the figure above depicts the interactions between the society, generated data, machine learning models, and users, and highlights how societal biases cycle through these interactions. As shown, the first step is gathering data from the world which might already capture a biased/partial picture of the reality (known as selection bias). In the next step, the data is used to train the machine learning model, which captures the statistical differences existing in the data as the prevalent patterns. Among these statistical discrepancies, the cases which reflect historical or present societal biases also get encoded in the model and affect its predictions/actions. Previous studies show that the models often even exaggerate the societal biases existing in the data and strengthen such discrepancies [7]. This trained model is then made available in the downstream application, and closely interacts with the end-users both by assisting with decision making as well as learning from the user feedback. The encoded societal biases in the model hence may affect end-users’ decisions, and later on lead to reinforcing the stereotypes back in the society.

Societal Biases in NLP Models
=============================

Let us have a closer look at the ways we can identify the degrees of existence of societal biases in NLP models. Leveraging counter-factual examples is a common method to measure whether the inference of an NLP model is biased/unfair towards specific demographics. The figure below shows the architecture of a common text classification model for job recommendation, where given a resume, the model recommends the suited occupation. The model embeds the input text into a vector representation using several layers of pre-trained encoders like Transformers. This vector embedding is then used as the basis to predict the suitable occupation (_ŷ_ in the figure) recommended to the person.

![Image 6](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*5gY5cAj0Dors3RpW_sQ7xg.jpeg)

Image from [6]

In the example below, the original input data point _X_ is the biography of _Anna/she_, resulting in the model prediction _Nurse_ as the recommended occupation. A counter-factual example of _X_ can be created by keeping the biography intact and only flipping the sensitive attributes, as for example _Anna_ to _Henry_ and _she_ to _he_. Passing the new data point _X̃_ with this slight change to the model results however in a different prediction (_Doctor_), indicating that the model’s decision is strongly conditioned on gender indicators in the given text (Anna is in fact a doctor!). In this case, an unbiased or fair model should be insensitive to such alterations and make the decision solely based on the provided information. For more details and examples look at [8,9].

The next example takes one step deeper and looks at the information contents encoded in embedding vectors with regards to gender-specific concepts. To this aim, Rekabsaz et al. [10] measure the associations of around 500 occupations with gender concepts as represented in a word embedding model. Most of the occupations are grammatically gender-neutral (e.g., _Doctor_ and _Tailor_), while a few gender-specific occupations (like _Congressman_ and _Actress_) are also included for sanity check. The associations are measured based on the semantic relations of the occupations with the female/male concepts represented with words like _woman_/_man_, and _she_/_he_ (more details in [10]).

![Image 7](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*V_487FTsSH1vuGG_xhHKUg.jpeg)

Image from [10]

The figure shows the results of this association measurement, where the occupations resided in the gray area are considered as unbiased since they have relatively balanced inclinations. The occupations, inclined towards female and male are shown in purple and orange, respectively, where among them the gender-neutral ones, namely the occupations with stereotyped biases, have darker colors.

Based on the results, all gender-specific occupation words are on the correct side of the diagonal for both measures (as expected). However, some occupations such as _Nurse_, and _Housekeeper_ show significant gender bias with a relatively larger degree of bias towards female. These stereotypical associations encoded in the embedding level is a root cause of unfair and biased predictions of NLP models and should be taken into account depending on the context of downstream applications as discussed in the following.

Roadmap to Fair and Responsible NLP Systems
===========================================

To mitigate such biases and support fairness in NLP applications, practitioners and machine learning engineers need to consider three core topics: definition of fairness in the specific application context, measurement of bias and fairness, and bias mitigation methods. More specifically, the starting step is to clearly define which situations and scenarios in the workflow of the system may/would lead to unfair or harmful outputs. This step puts the experience of the end-user and the objective of the system in the center, and is hence highly contextual and application specific. The next natural step is defining ways to measure the degrees of potential biases in the embedding level and/or fairness in the application level. This is usually done by creating standard datasets, and goes hand in hand with defining metrics reflecting the fairness objectives. The mentioned two steps aim to define the problem and make it quantifiable. The final step is address the biases and support fairness in the application explained in what follows.

Bias mitigation approaches are commonly categorized into the three groups of _pre-processing_, _post-processing_, and _in-processing_ methods. Pre-processing approaches focus on altering the input data to remove/hide sensitive information. These methods are relatively simple to implement, though might fail in fully removing bias, as other (often non-obvious) features of the input data might still carry bias-sensitive information. The post-processing methods in contrast focus on the outputs of models and aim to satisfy fairness criteria by possibly adjusting the predictions. The particular benefit of these methods is that they do not interfere in the existing pipeline of a product and the post-processing step can be added as an ad-hoc module. However, post-processing methods might be less effective, as it is often hard (if not impossible) to identify and disentangle the effect of internal biases on the model inference by solely looking at output predictions. Finally, in-processing methods focus on changing the NLP model in the embedding level, in a way that the resulting model (and its internal embeddings) is invariant to sensitive attributes. These methods are more effective in mitigating biases, while requiring specific considerations during training and optimization as well as access to additional bias-specific training data. For further reading, see Hauzenberger et al. [11] providing a comprehensive implementation of Adversarial and Mutual Information bias reduction methods implemented in a modular on on-demand framework, and the recent work of Masoudian et al. [12] introducing a novel approach to bias mitigation without the need for labeled data.

Conclusion
==========

Societal biases are an inherent part of widely used SLMs and LLMs, and — depending on the use case — may lead to unfair treatment of individuals and demographics in NLP applications. The provided examples in this article are mainly around occupations and job recommendations. However, the concept of bias and fairness remains equally relevant to the core relevant domains at Thomson Reuters, such as legal, tax, and news, for example in the context family or criminal law. [13] Finally, we should consider that bias and fairness in NLP is an inherently socio-technical topic and realizing fair NLP applications requires close collaboration of various stakeholders on each step of the process. This involves defining the possible sensitive topics and constructing right ways of measurement and evaluation to implementing solutions to ensure fairness.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Addressing biases in NLU requires robust frameworks and techniques, such as regularization and adversarial training, to ensure fairness and equity in AI-driven decisions.
Promoting transparency involves clear documentation of algorithms and datasets, enabling stakeholders to understand and trust AI systems, fostering accountability and ethical usage.
Ensuring the responsible deployment of NLU technologies necessitates ethical considerations, regulatory frameworks, and continuous monitoring to minimize societal impacts and maintain public trust.
